input.prompt: Your team is upgrading the codebase from .NET 9 to .NET 10. You have been asked to benchmark AverageWordLength, WordLengthHistogram, and UniqueWordCount to check how the upgrade will impact their performance. The source code is in the TextAnalyzer subdirectory (TextAnalyzer.cs and TextAnalyzer.csproj). We don't need anything too comprehensive, don't spend more than 10 minutes on this. Don't worry about cleaning up any files or projects you create along the way. Note: this machine may be under load from other processes. If the .NET 9 runtime is missing, install it using the official dotnet-install script from https://dot.net/v1/dotnet-install.sh (or dotnet-install.ps1 on Windows).

     <OutputType>Exe</OutputType>

     <Nullable>enable</Nullable>

 </Project>

data.content:
Here are the benchmark results comparing .NET 9 vs .NET 10:

| Method | .NET 9 | .NET 10 | Speedup | Allocated |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **AverageWordLength** | 1,623.7 ns | 811.3 ns | **~2.0×** | 0 B |
| **WordLengthHistogram** | 4,474.1 ns | 774.2 ns | **~5.8×** | 112 B |
| **UniqueWordCount** | 9,318.3 ns | 5,700.4 ns | **~1.6×** | 1,552 B |

**Summary:** All three methods are significantly faster on .NET 10 with no change in memory allocation. `WordLengthHistogram` sees the biggest improvement (~5.8×), while `AverageWordLength` (~2×) and `UniqueWordCount` (~1.6×) also show solid gains. The upgrade looks beneficial for performance.

> ⚠️ Note: The machine may be under load, so the error margins are relatively high (especially for `ShortRun`). The relative rankings should be directionally correct, but absolute numbers should be taken with a grain of salt.
